coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots cooperatively modulating photocatalytic performance and surface charge of O-doped g-C3N4 for efficient water disinfection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Xingxing
Sun, Jiadi
Sheng, Lina
Wang, Ziyue
Ye, Yongli
Zheng, Jiayu
Fan, Minghong
Zhang, Yinzhi
Sun, Xiulan
coredata.dc:description: The emergence of widespread microbial contamination and drug-resistant bacteria in the water environment poses a severe threat to public health. Photocatalysis is considered an efficient, energy-saving, and cost-effective disinfection strategy for effectively removing microbial contamination from water bodies. In this paper, a metal-free O-doped g-C3N4/carbon dots (O-CN/CDs) nanosheet photocatalysts are prepared in coordination with various modification strategies, which results in a considerable improvement in photocatalytic disinfection activity compared to bulk g-C3N4 (B-CN). First, O-doping and morphology modulation are achieved simultaneously with hydrothermal treatment, which not only hinders the recombination of photogenerated hole-electron pairs, but also enables the exposure of more active centers. Subsequently, loading of CDs onto O-CN nanosheets by electrostatic self-assembly increases the production of photogenerated hole-electron pairs by expanding the visible light absorption region and promoted the separation of photogenerated carriers by trapping photogenerated electrons. Interestingly, the loading of CDs changes the charge on the surface of the composite photocatalyst from negative to positive, making it easier for the active species to come in contact with bacteria, and thus improving bacterial disinfection performance. Under visible light irradiation, the inactivation efficiency of optimized O-CN/CDs against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) is log(C/C0) = 4.08, approximately 9 times higher than that of B-CN. The main active species inactivating bacteria are superoxide anions radicals (•O2 –) and photogenerated holes, and their attack causes damage to the membrane wall structure and leakage of intracellular components. Additionally, the feasibility of the as-prepared photocatalysts in water disinfection in real environments was confirmed by photocatalytic disinfection experiments in successive recovery cycles and in practical lake water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Water disinfection
Graphitic carbon nitride
Carbon dots
Electrostatic self-assembly

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of B-CN (a), O-CN (b), and O-CN/CDs-15 (c). HRTEM image of O-CN/CDs-15 (d). Zeta-potential of CDs, O-CN, and O-CN/CDs-x (e). N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and the corresponding pore size distribution curves (insert) of B-CN, O-CN, and O-CN/CDs-15 (f). XRD (g) and FT-IR (h) patterns of all photocatalysts.
High-resolution XPS spectra of N1s (a), O1s (b), and C1s (c).

UV–vis DRS (a) of all photocatalysts and band gap (b) of B-CN, O-CN, and O-CN/CDs-15. PL spectra (c) of all photocatalysts and time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra (d) of B-CN, O-CN, and O-CN/CDs-15. TPC (e) and EIS (f) of B-CN, O-CN, and O-CN/CDs-15.

(a) Inactivation efficiencies of as-prepared photocatalysts toward MRSA (3 × 106 CFU/mL) under visible-light irradiation. The content of K+ (b) and nucleic acids (c) in MASR suspensions treated with O-CN/CDs-15 for 4 h under visible-light irradiation. (d) Fluorescent images of live/dead MRSA. SEM and TEM images of MRSA cells before (e, f) and after (g, h) treatment with O-CN/CDs-15 for 4 h under visible-light irradiation.

(a) Photocatalytic disinfection ability of O-CN/CDs-15 with different scavengers under visible-light irradiation. ESR spectra of the (b) DMPO–•O2 – adducts and (c) DMPO–•OH adducts recorded with as-prepared photocatalysts under visible-light irradiation. (d) Schematic photocatalytic mechanism for the O-CN/CDs-15 under broad-spectrum light irradiation.

(a) Three-cycle run experiments of photocatalytic disinfection with O-CN/CDs-15 under visible-light irradiation. (b) XRD patterns of O-CN/CDs-15 before and after photocatalytic reaction. (c) Photocatalytic disinfection efficiency of O-CN/CDs-15 toward the bacteria in Lihu Lake water. (d) Photos of water samples taken from the Lihu Lake. (e) The growth of colonies of Lihu Lake water under different treatment conditions.
The synthesis process of the O-CN/CDs.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots cooperatively modulating photocatalytic performance and surface charge of O-doped g-C3N4 for efficient water disinfection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Product Quality Comprehensive Inspection and Testing Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Product Quality Comprehensive Inspection and Testing Center, Baoying 225800, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The emergence of widespread microbial contamination and drug-resistant bacteria in the water environment poses a severe threat to public health. Photocatalysis is considered an efficient, energy-saving, and cost-effective disinfection strategy for effectively removing microbial contamination from water bodies. In this paper, a metal-free O-doped g-C3N4/carbon dots (O-CN/CDs) nanosheet photocatalysts are prepared in coordination with various modification strategies, which results in a considerable improvement in photocatalytic disinfection activity compared to bulk g-C3N4 (B-CN). First, O-doping and morphology modulation are achieved simultaneously with hydrothermal treatment, which not only hinders the recombination of photogenerated hole-electron pairs, but also enables the exposure of more active centers. Subsequently, loading of CDs onto O-CN nanosheets by electrostatic self-assembly increases the production of photogenerated hole-electron pairs by expanding the visible light absorption region and promoted the separation of photogenerated carriers by trapping photogenerated electrons. Interestingly, the loading of CDs changes the charge on the surface of the composite photocatalyst from negative to positive, making it easier for the active species to come in contact with bacteria, and thus improving bacterial disinfection performance. Under visible light irradiation, the inactivation efficiency of optimized O-CN/CDs against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) is log(C/C0) = 4.08, approximately 9 times higher than that of B-CN. The main active species inactivating bacteria are superoxide anions radicals (•O2 –) and photogenerated holes, and their attack causes damage to the membrane wall structure and leakage of intracellular components. Additionally, the feasibility of the as-prepared photocatalysts in water disinfection in real environments was confirmed by photocatalytic disinfection experiments in successive recovery cycles and in practical lake water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Microbial contamination, caused by industrialization and rapid population growth is a constant threat to human health [1]. Specifically, the misuse of antibiotics has led to the emergence of drug-resistant bacteria [2], which has placed stringent requirements on the development of novel bacterial disinfection strategies. Photocatalytic disinfection is currently considered as a promising disinfection strategy [3] due to its high efficiency, energy efficiency and low cost. Nowadays, metal-based photocatalysts exhibit good photocatalytic disinfection performance, but they inevitably cause secondary contamination as due to heavy metal leakage during practical application [4–6]. As a result, developing efficient and green metal-free composite photocatalyst for bacterial disinfection in water pollution is crucial.

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), a new metal-free photocatalyst, generates electrons and holes under visible-light irradiation capable of redox reactions with O2 and •OH on its surface to generate a variety of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [7]. It is well known that ROS is a strong oxidizing agent with a wide range of microbial killing effects. Therefore, g-C3N4 is considered to be one of the most promising metal-free photocatalysts in the field of photocatalytic disinfection. However, pristine g-C3N4 still suffers from low visible-light utilization, fast photogenerated electron-hole recombination, and small specific surface area, which cannot meet practical application requirements when used as photocatalytic disinfectants [8,9]. Therefore, many modification strategies for enhancing the performance of g-C3N4 photocatalytic bacterial disinfection are proposed. For example, Nam Su Heo et al.[10] investigate the photocatalytic disinfection efficiency of g-C3N4 with different morphologies using Escherichia coli (E. coli), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Salmonella typhimurium as test strains, in which 2D g-C3N4 shows the best photocatalytic disinfection performance. Lin et al.[11] find that boron doping can enhance the photocatalytic inactivation efficiency of g-C3N4 against E. coli in the near-infrared light range. Therefore, it is feasible to use morphological modulation and elemental doping to improve the photocatalytic bacterial disinfection performance of g-C3N4 in a synergistic manner.

The composite of g-C3N4 with other semiconductors can also substantially enhance photocatalytic performance as the photoinduced electrons and holes can cross the coupling interface of the composite photocatalyst and thus achieve spatial separation [12–14]. CDs are commonly used as a semiconductor carbon-based material for the modification of g-C3N4 due to their exceptional physicochemical and optoelectronic properties (electrical conductivity, low toxicity, photosensitivity, optical stability and up-conversion photoluminescence) [15,16]. The embedding of CDs not only substantially enhances the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4, but also ensures the low toxicity of the composite photocatalyst without secondary contamination in practical applications. Wang et al.[17] use an ultrasound-assisted method to obtain sulfur-doped CDs/g-C3N4 hollow tubular composite photocatalysts that perform well in the photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline and inactivation of E. coli. Similarly, Tang et al.[18] synthesize CDs/g-C3N4 composite photocatalysts using an impregnation method, which remarkably enhances the photocatalytic inactivation of S. aureus compared to g-C3N4. Additionally, it is reported that a tight coupling interface can be formed between the oppositely charged CDs and g-C3N4, which is beneficial for extending the lifetime of photogenerated carriers and hindering the recombination of electron-hole pairs [19]. Therefore, electrostatic self-assembly of g-C3N4/CDs composite photocatalyst with a tighter coupling interface is a worthwhile design strategy for improving photocatalytic bacterial disinfection performance.

Motivated by the above considerations, we obtain O-CN porous nanosheets by hydrothermal treatment of B-CN in the presence of H2O2, which simultaneously achieves elemental doping and morphology modulation of B-CN. Subsequently, the O-CN/CDs composite photocatalysts are successfully prepared by loading positively charged CDs on O-CN via electrostatic self-assembly. The photocatalytic enhancement mechanism of this design strategy is investigated by characterizing the morphological structure, elemental composition, and optoelectronic properties of the composite photocatalyst. The photocatalytic disinfection performance of O-CN/CDs is further evaluated using MRSA as a model biohazard, and the bacterial disinfection mechanism of this photocatalytic system is preliminarily elucidated. Finally, the application potential of the photocatalysts in the field of photocatalytic water disinfection in practical environment was explored.

The CDs were prepared based on previous work [20]. Typically, 30 mL of deionized water was used to dissolve 0.2 g of 2,5-diaminophenyl sulfonic acid. This solution was then put into a 50 mL autoclave, heated for 8 h at 200 °C, and cooled naturally to room temperature. After the reaction, the red-brown solution was centrifuged at 14000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was dialyzed against deionized water in a 500 Da dialysis bag for 24 h.

B-CN is synthesized by calcining urea in a crucible at 550 °C for 4 h at a heating rate of 2.5 °C/min in a muffle furnace. Subsequently, 1 g of B-CN was dispersed in 200 mL of 30% H2O2 aqueous solution and sonicated for 30 min. The suspension was transferred to a 50 mL autoclave, heated at 130 °C for 6 h, and then cooled to room temperature [21]. Finally, the hydrothermally treated suspension was centrifuged at 8000 rpm to collect the precipitate and washed thoroughly with deionized water and freeze-dried to obtain O-CN powder.

CDs and O-CN were compounded by electrostatic self-assembly. First, 0.1 g of O-CN was dispersed sonication-assisted in 85 mL of deionized water, and then 15 mL of CDs solution was added dropwise under magnetic stirring at 500 rpm. Subsequently, after continuous stirring in the dark for 24 h, the suspension was centrifuged at 8000 rpm to collect the precipitate and rinsed thoroughly with deionized water. Finally, the precipitate was freeze-dried to obtain O-CN/CDs-x (x = 5, 10, 15, 20 mL), x being the volume of the aqueous CDs solution.

MRSA was used as a biocontamination model for the assessment of bacterial disinfection performance of photocatalysts. The bacteria were proliferated in LB medium at 37 °C for 12 h. The bacterial suspension was then enriched by centrifugation at 3500 rpm and washed three times with 0.85% saline. The bacterial suspension was then diluted to approximately 3 × 107 CFU/mL and set aside. In the photocatalytic disinfection experiments, 6 mg of O-CN/CDs were sonicated in 27 mL of saline, to which 3 mL of the diluted bacterial suspension was added to adjust the final cell density and photocatalyst concentration to approximately 3 × 106 CFU/mL and 0.2 mg/mL, respectively. Finally, the reaction system was exposed to a light source of 350 W xenon lamp (λ > 420 nm) for photocatalytic bacterial disinfection experiments. And at different time intervals, 1 mL of the treated bacterial solution was collected for plate colony counting to determine the number of live bacteria in the reaction system after different irradiation times. For comparison, the control experiments were carried out in the dark using the photocatalysts and in the absence of the photocatalysts in the light. Each test was performed in triplicate for error estimation.

The preparation procedure of O-CN/CDs composite photocatalysts is shown in Scheme 1 . CDs are synthesized by a typical hydrothermal process (Figs. S1,2), while O-CN is prepared by a calcination-hydrothermal two-step process. The modification of O-CN by CDs is achieved under electrostatic forces due to the different charges on their surfaces. As shown in Fig. 1 e, the average Zeta-potentials of CDs and O-CN are 17.43 mV and −7.45 mV, respectively, and the Zeta-potential of the composite photocatalyst increases with the increase of CDs loading on O-CN. This result indicates that electrostatic self-assembly can occur between O-CN and CDs, and tentatively confirms the successful compounding between them. It should be noted that Wang et al.[22] demonstrated that the increase in positive charge on the surface of the CDs is related to the pyrrole and pyridine nitrogen in their structures. Along with our previous findings [20], it is clear that the considerable positive charge on the surface of the as-prepared CDs may also be attributed to the formation of pyrrole and pyridine nitrogen. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images provide a better insight into the preparation process of the composite photocatalysts. B-CN has a lamellar stacking structure (Fig. 1a), whereas O-CN has a loose porous lamellar structure with visible nanosheet transparency in the edge areas (Fig. 1b), confirming the effectiveness of liquid-phase sonication and H2O2 oxidative corrosion process (Figs. S3,4) [21]. The morphology of O-CN/CDs maintains the porous nanosheet structure of O-CN and the presence of spherical CDs is observed in some regions (Fig. 1c,d), further confirming the successful preparation of the composite photocatalysts. Furthermore, the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore size distributions of B-CN, O-CN and O-CN/CDs are shown in Fig. 1f, which show typical type IV isotherms, confirming the mesoporous structure of the as-prepared photocatalysts [23]. The BET specific surface area (SBET) of O-CN is as high as 121.50 m2/g, about 1.4 times higher than the SBET of B-CN (85.80 m2/g), which fully corroborates the pore structure observed in the TEM images. The introduction of CDs reduces the SBET of O-CN/CDs-15 to 82.77 m2/g, which may be attributed to the space occupation caused by the successful loading of CDs [24]. Interestingly, the pore size distribution of O-CN and O-CN/CDs is remarkably smaller than that of B-CN, indicating that the H2O2 thermal oxidation treatment has exceptional porogenic capacity. Photocatalysts with both mesoporous structures and high specific surface area can shorten transport pathways of carriers from the interior to the surface and offer more active sites for photocatalytic reactions [25–28].

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns in Fig. 1g show the crystal structures of the B-CN, O-CN and O-CN/CDs samples. In agreement with previous studies, two distinct diffraction peaks are found in the XRD patterns of all samples: a stronger peak at 27.5° and a weaker peak at 13.3°, which corresponds to the interlayer stacking of the aromatic structure ((002) plane) and the in-plane structural stacking of the tri-s-triazine units ((100) plane), respectively [29,30]. However, the peak intensities of (100) and (002) are enhanced in O-CN and O-CN/CDs compared to B-CN, implying that the hydrothermal treatment of H2O2 leads to more ordered interlayer stacking within the g-C3N4 framework [24]. Furthermore, the crystal structure of O-CN/CDs remains almost identical to that of O-CN due to the poor crystallinity of CDs and their limited loading [31]. As observed in Fig. 1h, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of all samples show characteristic absorption peaks at 808 cm−1 and 1250–1750 cm−1, respectively corresponding to bending vibration of triazine unit and stretching vibration of CN heterocyclic ring [17], indicating that the structural units of g-C3N4 are well maintained during the preparation. It is noteworthy to mention that there are some differences in the distribution of the characteristic absorption peaks of B-CN, O-CN and O-CN/CDs between 3000 and 3600 cm−1, which may be related to the hydrothermal treatment and the loading of the CDs.

The chemical structures and elemental compositions of B-CN, O-CN, and O-CN/CDs are further characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). In the full XPS spectra, B-CN and O-CN are composed mainly of C, N, and O, and few S elements from CDs are present in O-CN/CDs (Fig. S5). More importantly, the C/N atomic ratio in O-CN/CDs (0.98) is marginally higher than that of O-CN (0.86), which again validates the embedding of CDs. The high-resolution XPS spectra of B-CN, O-CN and O-CN/CDs are shown in Fig. 2 . The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2a) shows four deconvolution peaks, which are C–NC (398.6 eV), N–(C)3 (399.8 eV), N-Hx (401.3 eV) and the π-π* excitation (404.4 eV) of the CN conjugate structure [32]. The deconvolution peak of C–NC in O-CN is shifted towards higher binding energy compared to B-CN, indicating that the sp2-hybridised N can be replaced by a more electronegative O atom during the H2O2 hydrothermal treatment [33]. The intensity of the deconvolution peak corresponding to CO (532.0 eV) in O-CN is remarkably higher than that of B-CN [34], further confirming that H2O2 treatment introduces more O-containing groups (Fig. 2b). The deconvolution of the C1s spectrum produces four characteristic peaks centered at 284.6 eV, 286.0 eV, 288.1 eV, and 288.9 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CN/CS, N–CN, and CO (Fig. 2c) [35,36]. The intensity of the CC/CC peaks in O-CN/CDs is slightly higher than that of O-CN, indicating the successful introduction of graphitic carbon species in CDs [37]. It should be noted that the stronger characteristic peaks in B-CN centered at 284.6 eV and 286.0 eV are due to incomplete reactions of the precursors during calcination. Additionally, weaker C–SOx (168.3 eV) and CS (163.5 eV) characteristic peaks are observed in the S2p spectra (Fig. S6) of O-CN/CDs [20,38], which further demonstrates the successful fabrication of the O-CN/CDs photocatalysts.

The introduction of CDs as photosensitizers into O-CN can efficiently change the optical properties and light absorption performance of the composite photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. S7, with the increase of CDs loading (a-f), the color of the composite photocatalysts gradually changes from light yellow to dark brown, which implies the enhancement of their visible-light absorption. To test this conclusion, all photocatalysts are characterized using UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS). As shown in Fig. 3 a, the absorption edges of both O-CN and O-CN/CDs show a slight red shift compared to B-CN and a significant enhancement of the absorption peaks from the UV to near-infrared region, indicating that both the doping of O elements and the embedding of CDs are efficient in enhancing the photoresponse of the photocatalysts. The band gap energies of B-CN, O-CN, and O-CN/CDs-15 are calculated by the Tauc method to be 2.63 eV, 2.59 eV, and 2.52 eV, respectively (Fig. 3b) [39,40]. The decreasing band gap energy represents the photocatalysts with better visible-light capture and the ability to produce more photogenerated carriers [24]. The separation and complexation of photogenerated carriers are investigated afterwards using steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy and time-resolved fluorescence decay spectroscopy. In Fig. 3c, it can be observed that the fluorescence emission peak of O-CN is slightly lower than that of B-CN under the excitation light irradiation at 375 nm, which indicates that the H2O2 hydrothermal treatment hinders the recombination of photogenerated carriers. This phenomenon may be attributed to the fact that the O-doping changes the electronic structure of B-CN, and the formation of the porous structure decreases the carrier recombination possibility [41]. Furthermore, as the loading of CDs increases, the PL intensity of the composite photocatalysts first decreases then increases, and the fluorescence intensity of O-CN/CDs-15 is the lowest, thus indicating that the loading of CDs in O-CN/CDs-15 is optimal. These results suggest that the exceptional induced electron transfer properties of CDs can further facilitate the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which is consistent with previous reports [19]. Fluorescence decay curves are fitted to obtain fluorescence lifetimes of 10.55 ns, 5.15 ns, and 3.64 ns respectively for B-CN, O-CN, and O-CN/CDs-15 (Fig. 3d). The significantly shorter fluorescence lifetimes indicate that efficient electron transfer occurs in the composite photocatalysts [24]. To further assess the separation behavior of photogenerated carriers visually, the transient photocurrent density (TPC) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of B-CN, O-CN, and O-CN/CDs-15 are investigated. Under visible-light irradiation, it can be observed in Fig. 3e that the highest photocurrent response is detected for O-CN/CDs-15, followed by O-CN, and finally B-CN at every on–off light cycle. Fig. 3f shows the EIS of the three photocatalysts with the order of the arc radius of the Nyquist plot: O-CN/CDs-15 < O-CN < B-CN. These results fully confirm that the O doping and the introduction of CDs can significantly promote the separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs to enhance the photocatalytic activity of the composite photocatalysts.

The photocatalytic bacterial disinfection performance of the as-prepared photocatalysts is evaluated under visible-light irradiation using MRSA as a bacterial model. As shown in Fig. 4 a, under dark conditions, there is no obvious decrease in the number of viable bacteria in all photocatalytic systems, indicating that these photocatalysts themselves have no bactericidal effect (Fig. S8). However, under visible-light irradiation, the number of viable bacteria in the photocatalyst-treated groups gradually decreases with the prolongation of the irradiation time. Under visible-light irradiation, only about 0.46-log and 1.13-log reduction of MRSA cell density by B-CN and O-CN are achieved, while the control group shows almost no significant change in cell density. Besides, O-CN/CDs-15 can reduce the cell density of MRSA by 4.08-log, and its remarkable bacterial disinfection performance is consistent with the previous characterization results of photoelectric properties (Fig. S9). Moreover, the photocatalytic bactericidal efficiency increases with the prolongation of irradiation time, which may be due to the continuous accumulation of ROS generated by photocatalysis in the reaction system. Subsequently, the used concentration of O-CN/CDs-15 is optimized since the change of the photocatalyst concentration will affect the photocatalytic performance. With the increase of the used concentration, the photocatalytic bacterial inactivation efficiency of O-CN/CDs-15 gradually increases and then decreases, as shown in Fig. S10. When the concentration is 0.2 g/L, the photocatalytic system shows the best bacterial inactivation performance. Excessive photocatalyst concentration may lead to insufficient light exposure of the reaction system, resulting in low photocatalytic disinfection efficiency. Therefore, determining the appropriate photocatalyst concentration is also critical to photocatalytic disinfection efficiency.

To verify the damaging effect of O-CN/CDs-15 towards MRSA during photocatalysis, fluorescence-based live/dead bacterial staining tests were conducted. The SYTO 9 dye penetrated the intact cell membrane and labelled live bacteria with green fluorescence, whereas the PI dye only penetrated the damaged cell membrane and caused the dead bacteria to fluoresce red. As shown in Fig. 4d, almost all bacteria in the control group were labelled by green fluorescence, indicating that their membrane wall structure was not damaged, which is consistent with the results of the plate colony counting study. In contrast, the vast majority of bacteria in the fluorescence images of photocatalytic treatment group exhibited intense red fluorescence, suggesting that the membrane wall structure of MRSA was disrupted in the presence of the O-CN/CDs-15 photocatalysts. Additionally, an interesting phenomenon was found in the confocal images of the photocatalytic treatment group: almost all bacteria were adhered to the O-CN/CDs-15 photocatalysts, which is due to the O-CN/CDs-15 carrying opposite charges to the bacterial surface. The electrostatic interaction between bacteria and photocatalyst may increase the contact of active species with bacteria, which can promote the bactericidal effect of ROS. The changes in morphology and membrane structure of MRSA before and after photocatalytic treatment are characterized to further observe the damage to MRSA membrane wall structure by photocatalytic treatment. It can be observed in Fig. 4e that MRSA without photocatalytic treatment was spherical and its cellular structure is intact. However, after 4 h of photocatalytic treatment, the cell wall of the bacteria is damaged, and the intracellular components severely leak (Fig. 4g), resulting in the death of the bacteria. At the same time, the SEM images of MRSA before and after photocatalytic treatment are compared (Fig. 4f,h), and obvious shrinkage, depression and perforation appear on the surface of the bacteria after photocatalytic treatment. The above phenomenon indicates that the active species generated by the photocatalysts inactivate bacteria mainly by attacking the cell wall [42]. Adhesion between the photocatalysts and the bacteria was likewise found from the SEM images. Subsequently, we measured the contents of K+ and nucleic acids in the bacterial supernatant after the photocatalytic treatment. As shown in Fig. 4b and c, with the increase of photocatalytic treatment time, the contents of K+ and nucleic acids in the bacterial supernatant showed an increasing trend, which sufficiently proved that photocatalytic treatment could change the permeability of the cell membrane and induce the leakage of intracellular components.

The active species capture experiment is carried out to better explain the possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism of bacterial inactivation. Isopropanol (IPA), ascorbic acid (AA), and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) are selected as scavengers (The concentration is 1 mM) for hydroxyl radicals (•OH), •O2 –, and photogenerated holes, respectively [43]. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the bacterial disinfection performance of the photocatalysts changes only slightly after adding IPA, indicating that the role of •OH in the bacterial inactivation process is negligible. The photocatalytic bacterial disinfection activity is inhibited in the presence of either the scavenger AA or EDTA-2Na, and the inhibition effect of AA is the most significant. These results indicate that •O2 – is the main active species for photocatalytic inactivation of MRSA, followed by photogenerated holes. Due to the short lifetime of photogenerated holes, it is not able to diffuse and accumulate sufficiently in the photocatalytic reaction system, resulting in a weaker bacterial inactivation performance. Electron spin resonance (ESR) characterization of B-CN, O-CN and O-CN/CDs-15 is then carried out using DMPO as an electron spin trapping agent to confirm the generation of •O2 –. The characteristic peaks of the DMPO–•O2 – adducts are all detected under visible-light irradiation as shown in Fig. 5b, indicating that all three photocatalysts are able to produce •O2 –. It is worth noting that the order of strength of the •O2 – characteristic peaks in the ESR patterns of three photocatalysts is: O-CN/CDs-15 > O-CN > B-CN, implying that the hydrothermal treatment of H2O2 and the loading of CDs can synergistically promote the generation of •O2 –. Therefore, the increased bacterial disinfection activity of O-CN/CDs-15 is mainly attributed to the generation of more active species under visible-light irradiation. Moreover, the signal peak of •OH in the O-CN/CDs-15 photocatalysts is similarly enhanced (Fig. 5c). Although the •OH plays a minimal role in the photocatalytic inactivation of MRSA, the enhancement of its signal peak may also reflect the enhanced photocatalytic performance of O-CN/CDs-15 photocatalysts.

The flat band potential of the O-CN/CDs-15 photocatalysts were subsequently measured by Mott-Schottky plots. A positive tangent slope is shown in the Mott-Schottky curve (Fig. S11), which implies that O-CN/CDs-15 is an n-type semiconductor [44]. For n-type semiconductors, the conduction band (CB) position coincides with the flat band potential, so the CB potential of O-CN/CDs-15 was determined to be −0.84 V (vs. standard hydrogen electrode, pH 7.0) [45,46]. The band gap energy of O-CN/CDs-15 is known to be 2.53 eV (Fig. 3b), so the valence band (VB) potential of O-CN/CD-15 is calculated to be 1.68 eV using the equation ECB = EVB - Eg, where EVB, ECB and Eg are VB potential, CB potential and the band gap energy, respectively [17,24]. A possible photocatalytic mechanism for the O-CN/CDs photocatalytic disinfection system is proposed based on the above experimental results, as shown in Fig. 5d. Under simulated sunlight irradiation, the embedding of CDs enables O-CN/CDs-15 to absorb visible and near-infrared light directly, and the broadened spectral absorption increases the generation of electrons and holes in the CB and VB of the composite photocatalysts. The porous sheet structure of O-CN can expose more active centers compared to B-CN, which efficiently shortens the diffusion path of photogenerated carriers from interior to surface. The doping of O atoms also alters the electron density distribution of B-CN, thus limiting photogenerated hole-electron pairs recombination. Furthermore, CDs act as electron mediators that can effectively trap photogenerated electrons, which substantially facilitates the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The electrons enriched in the CB of the O-CN/CDs-15 photocatalysts can easily react with O2 in the solution to generate •O2 –, and then attack MRSA to inactivate it. On the other hand, the photogenerated holes in the VB of O-CN/CDs-15 photocatalysts can directly participate in photocatalytic bacterial disinfection. In conclusion, in the O-CN/CDs-15 photocatalytic system, •O2 – and holes as the main active species can attack MRSA, which causes damage to the membrane wall structure and cytoplasmic leakage to achieve photocatalytic disinfection of pathogenic bacteria in the aqueous environment.

To further verify the application potential of O-CN/CDs-15 photocatalysts in practical water disinfection, we first investigated its recyclability. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the bactericidal performance of O-CN/CDs-15 only slightly decreases after three consecutive photocatalytic disinfection cycles, indicating that the composite photocatalyst has good stability and recyclability over three use cycles. This conclusion is further confirmed by measuring the XRD patterns of O-CN/CDs-15 before and after the photocatalytic disinfection reaction (Fig. 6b). Subsequently, we collected water samples from the actual environment and used O-CN/CDs-15 photocatalysts for the photocatalytic disinfection of the water sample (Fig. 6d). As shown in Fig. 6c and e, the number of viable bacteria in practical water sample with the addition of O-CN/CDs-15 photocatalysts gradually decreases with the prolongation of the irradiation time. After 4 h of photocatalytic treatment, the viable bacteria in the actual water samples were almost completely inactivated. In contrast, neither visible light irradiation nor photocatalysts treatment showed significant disinfection effects. The above evidence suggests that O-CN/CDs-15 can be applied as a metal-free composite photocatalysts for photocatalytic disinfection of practical environmental water.

This work prepared a metal-free O-CN/CDs photocatalyst for photocatalytic bacterial disinfection by electrostatic self-assembly. The photocatalytic inactivation efficiency of O-CN/CDs (4.08-log) for MRSA was 9 times higher than that of B-CN (0.46-log). The excellent bactericidal performance of O-CN/CDs was mainly attributed to the enhancement of photoelectric conversion efficiency and the modulation of surface charge. On the one hand, the embedding of CDs not only broadens the visible light absorption range of the composite photocatalysts, but also promotes the separation of photogenerated carriers by trapping photogenerated electrons. On the other hand, CDs can change the surface charge of O-CN and promote the bacterial-photocatalyst adhesion via electrostatic attraction, improving the effective usage of the active species for bacterial inactivation. Additionally, it was found that •O2 – and holes were the main active species during the photocatalytic disinfection process. The active species can attack the bacteria causing damage to the cellular structure, leakage of cell contents and eventually cell death. We also conducted photocatalytic disinfection experiments using actual lake water samples, which proved the practical value of O-CN/CDs. Therefore, this study not only provides an efficient and metal-free composite photocatalyst for bacterial disinfection in aqueous environments, but also provides some ideas for developing new metal-free g-C3N4-based photocatalysts.

Xingxing Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jiadi Sun: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Lina Sheng: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Ziyue Wang: Investigation, Methodology. Yongli Ye: Writing – review & editing, Software. Jiayu Zheng: Resources. Minghong Fan: Validation. Yinzhi Zhang: Project administration. Xiulan Sun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the key Research and Development Program of Yunnan Province, China (202202AE090007), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province, China (No. BK20190584), the Fellowship China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M671343), the Science and Technology Project of Jiangsu Provincial Bureau of Market Supervision and Administration, China (KJ2022054), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (JUSRP222001), and the Collaborative Innovation Center of Food Safety and Quality Control in Jiangsu Province, Jiangnan University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.11.002.
